scenario: Granite Insurer prepares a Camunda 8.8 review checklist for 12 BPMN models, each handling around 5 messages per integration. The architect must record which of the four response-handling patterns the integration guidance explicitly recommends to avoid, so that reviewers can flag it during a 2 hours walkthrough scheduled next week.
question: Which of the four response-handling patterns does the integration guidance recommend reviewers try to avoid?

options.text: The event-based gateway pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid because it is hard to understand for non-BPMN professionals and it also hides the happy path of the flow
The receive task with boundary message events pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid even though it keeps the happy path of the flow easy to see on the diagram
The payload field plus XOR gateway pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid in every model that handles more than one type of response message reply
The event subprocess pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid because once the flow moves into the subprocess it cannot easily return to the typical flow

a.text: Correct. The Handling different response messages table assigns the recommendation "Try to avoid" only to the event-based gateway, calling out that it is hard to understand for non-BPMN audiences and hides the happy path.

b.text: Incorrect. The receive-task-with-boundary-events row recommends using it when message types should be visible on the diagram, capped at two boundary events; it is not the one to avoid.

c.text: Incorrect. The payload + XOR gateway row is recommended for many response types or when the response can be treated as a result, not avoided.

d.text: Incorrect. Event subprocesses are recommended for bigger scopes that can react to events at any time; the docs flag a known limitation but do not place them in the avoid bucket.

explanation: In the Handling different response messages table, only the event-based gateway carries the "Try to avoid" recommendation because it is hard to understand for non-BPMN audiences and obscures the happy path. The other three patterns are recommended in their respective contexts.
